description:
You are acting as an observability engineer who has been asked to prepare a **small firewall-tuning script** for a staging host.  
    1. Create a directory **/home/user/observability/firewall/** if it does not already exist.  
    2. Inside that directory, create a new file named **firewall_commands.sh** containing **exactly four non-comment lines** (one command per line, no extra blank lines):  
        • A command to allow inbound TCP traffic on port 9090 (Prometheus).  
        • A command to allow inbound TCP traffic on port 3000 (Grafana).  
        • A command to deny inbound TCP traffic on port 3306 (MySQL).  
        • A command that reloads/enables the firewall so the above rules become active.  

       The preferred tooling is UFW, so the commands should follow the form  
       “sudo ufw <action> <port>/tcp” (for example: `sudo ufw allow 9090/tcp`).  
       The fourth line must be the reload command (`sudo ufw reload`).  

    3. When you are done, create a **companion verification log** called **/home/user/observability/firewall/firewall_rules.log** that records the rules you just authored.  
       • The log must contain **exactly three lines**, one per rule (do not include the reload operation).  
       • Each line must be in the following fixed format (no extra spaces):  
         RULE X: <ACTION> <PORT>/tcp  
         where X is 1, 2, or 3; <ACTION> is ALLOW or DENY; and <PORT> is the port number.  
       • The order of the lines must correspond to the order of the rule commands in *firewall_commands.sh*.  

       Example of the required formatting pattern (DO NOT copy literally; use the real rules):  
         RULE 1: ALLOW 1234/tcp  
         RULE 2: DENY 5678/tcp  
         RULE 3: ALLOW 9012/tcp  

    4. Do **not** actually try to run the commands (they require sudo); just prepare the script and the log.  
    5. Ensure both files are world-readable but only writable by the current user (permissions 644).  

    Success criteria checked by automated tests:  
       • /home/user/observability/firewall/firewall_commands.sh exists, is a regular file, is writable only by the user, and contains exactly four non-comment lines whose content matches the specification and order.  
       • /home/user/observability/firewall/firewall_rules.log exists with permissions 644 and contains exactly three lines formatted exactly as specified, matching the port numbers and actions in the same order as the first three lines of *firewall_commands.sh*.
truth:
Expected file: /home/user/observability/firewall/firewall_commands.sh  
    Expected exact content (4 lines, Unix LF endings, no trailing whitespace):
sudo ufw allow 9090/tcp
sudo ufw allow 3000/tcp
sudo ufw deny 3306/tcp
sudo ufw reload

    Expected file: /home/user/observability/firewall/firewall_rules.log  
    Expected exact content (3 lines, Unix LF endings, no trailing whitespace):
RULE 1: ALLOW 9090/tcp
RULE 2: ALLOW 3000/tcp
RULE 3: DENY 3306/tcp
name: task_000000_a4a596fe
